answer:
**Manu's foundational claim on Brahmins and *dakṣiṇā***: In the *Manusmṛti*, Brahmins are entitled to receive sacrificial fees (*dakṣiṇā*, often translated as "gifts" from pure sources) as one of their six authorized occupations, particularly through performing sacrifices (*yajana*) for others—a role denied to lower *varṇas*. This positions Brahmins as primary recipients, as their ritual labor justifies the transfer of goods (cattle, wealth) from patrons without the Brahmin having physically produced or occupied the items.[^1] The text emphasizes purity in such receipts, distinguishing Brahminic service from Vaishya labor (trade, agriculture) or Shudra service.[^2]

**Comte's foundational claim on property from labor**: Property originates in the "occupation" (*occupation des choses*) of unowned things—taking physical possession with intent to appropriate and modify them via industry (*travail*, labor)—transforming raw matter into useful goods. This stems from natural liberty, where one's faculties create value without harming others; no prior owner exists, and labor (not ritual or gift) confers exclusive right.[^3] Liberty is the precondition, as slavery (treating persons as property) blocks such productive occupation.[^4] Labor here means physical/economic appropriation (e.g., cultivating land, crafting tools), yielding utility measured by services rendered.[^5]

**The clash**: These traditions fundamentally diverge on *what entitles ownership*. Manu roots legitimate transfer in ritual hierarchy: a thing "belongs" to the Brahmin via *dakṣiṇā* as recompense for spiritual service (*yajana*), embedding property in *varṇa*-dharma where Brahmins claim goods produced by others' physical labor (e.g., Kshatriya/ Vaishya patrons) without direct occupation.[^6] Comte insists ownership arises solely from *one's own* labor/occupation on res nullius (unowned things), rejecting unearned transfers like gifts-as-fee unless from voluntary exchange of self-produced value; ritual "service" adds no proprietary claim without physical transformation.[^7]

The core tension is *about what counts as labor*: Manu's "labor" is sacerdotal (Vedic recitation, sacrifice mediation), non-productive on the material itself, divinely ordained, and hierarchical (Brahmin-superior). Comte's is material/productive (occupation yielding utility), egalitarian (any free agent), and natural (liberty-derived). It secondarily concerns *ownership basis*—caste-ritual gift (*dāna* to Brahmins) vs. self-appropriation—but resolves to labor's definition, as Manu's system treats ritual as value-creating (justifying unearned property), while Comte sees only industry transforming nature as such.[^8]

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 10, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-4a77ef).
[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 10, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-f85712).
[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chapter 4, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-ab8596).
[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chapter 2, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-694e44).
[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chapter 5, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/05-de-lappropriation-des-choses-en-general/translation/#p-e68210).
[^6]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 10, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-897899) (Brahmin superiority in rites/gifts).
[^7]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chapter 4, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-8094b8).
[^8]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 10, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-b34355); cf. Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chapter 5, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/05-de-lappropriation-des-choses-en-general/translation/#p-1db575).

args.query: dakṣiṇā brāhmana OR brahmana OR brahmin OR "priest"
"sacrificial fee" OR daksina OR dakṣiṇā brahmin OR brahmana OR brāhmana OR priest Manu OR Manusmṛti
"property" (labour OR labor OR work OR travail OR industry OR occupation)
dakṣiṇā OR daksina OR "sacrificial fee" OR "priest fee" OR "gift to brahmin" Manu OR Manusmṛti
"occupation" OR labour OR labor OR travail property OR propriété
